source: Destination Trail medical director email — Tahoe 200 Full Loop (May 2026)

sleep_guidance.start_by: Night 2 — ideally within 24–40 hours of the start
sleep_guidance.nap_benefit: Even 30–60 minutes can dramatically improve safety and mental function
sleep_guidance.pull_risk: Severe delusion or unsafe function may result in being pulled from the race

sections.title: General safety
bullets: Extreme temperature swings — hot, cold, windy, dusty, and everything in between.
Fix problems before leaving an aid station.
Sleep, food, hydration, and dry clothing solve most issues.
Carry more layers and calories than you think you'll need.
Be proactive, not reactive.

sections.title: Helping fellow runners
bullets: If someone asks for help, do what you can.
If a runner needs assistance or rescue, gather bib number (photo if possible), location, and issue.
Do not disturb runners sleeping safely off-trail.
If someone is on or immediately next to the trail, check on them.

sections.title: Heat illness & overheating
bullets: Watch for nausea, confusion, red or dry skin, or lack of sweating.
Get into shade, cool gradually, wet clothing and skin, sip fluids slowly, eat salty foods.
If shivering begins while cooling, stop aggressive cooling and warm slightly.

sections.title: When to call for help
bullets: Unless immediately life-threatening, contact race staff before calling 911 — staff can often reach you faster.
Most rescues require walking out, even with medical support.
Helicopter rescues are for extreme, life-threatening emergencies only.
Rescue wait times may be 4–24 hours; weather and terrain delay response.
Rescue or medical costs outside Destination Trail operations are the runner's responsibility.

sections.title: Cold weather & hypothermia
bullets: Always carry extra warm and waterproof gear (mandatory kit).
Conditions change rapidly with elevation and time of day.
If hypothermic: add layers, eat calories, keep moving if safely possible.

sections.title: Dust & Rubicon cough
bullets: Full loop includes the Rubicon — miles of extremely fine powder-like dust.
Consider a reusable mask, several disposable masks, and a wet buff.
Practice face coverings before race day — some feel restrictive when climbing.
OTC meds like Mucinex may help; test before race day.

sections.title: If you get lost
bullets: Use your GPX track, keep devices charged, stay on trail — no cross-country shortcuts.
If truly lost: stop moving, stay put, conserve battery, wait for assistance.

sections.title: Sleep deprivation & hallucinations
bullets: Can cause confusion, poor decisions, hallucinations, and dangerous behavior.
Begin sleeping no later than Night 2 (ideally within 24–40 hours of start).
Communicate with staff if struggling mentally or physically.

sections.title: Foot care essentials
bullets: You are responsible for your foot care — manage blisters and moisture early.
Address hot spots immediately; waiting for the next aid is a poor choice over 200 miles.
Let feet breathe and dry at aids when eating or sleeping — pays off later.
Wear gaiters — fine dust on course will punish unprotected feet.
